content: I would say one of my greatest strengths as an employee is my ability to recognize areas for improvement and processes and my willingness to speak up about them. I'm good at identifying gaps in procedures or inefficiencies and creating workarounds that allow myself and other members of my team to get work done not only quicker but more accurately and more efficiently. Equally as important I found is my willingness to speak up about those kinds of things. I found that even people who are also good at that are reluctant to speak up in team meetings or even in one-on-one situations with management for being perceived as rebellious or going beyond their role or their expectations. But I found if it's done in a respectful manner where your motive is clearly to help the team and not to point out flaws for the sake of pointing out flaws that management not only tolerates it but appreciates it. It's consistently one of the things on my employee evaluations that's called out as a positive. And weakness can kind of go hand in hand with that for me because in situations where I do that and my advice is either ignored or rebuffed in some way. I can kind of take it personally. I try not to let that show. I try to respectfully accept whatever decision is made. But it can kind of eat at me when I really think that I'm right about a process matter and my advice just isn't being seriously considered. content: When I join a new team, I try to do a lot more listening than I do talking. I always go out of my way to be affable and introduce myself to people and not be standoffish or too reserved. But when it comes to talking about things relevant to the job, I just try to hang back and listen to the way the people training me do things and try to follow the training program that they put in place. I'm always very active participant in the training process when there are opportunities for participation. But I find that it's much better when you're brand new to a team that you kind of show them the respect of being willing to learn the way they're doing things without having too much input other than the input that's asked for. As I mentioned in another answer, I'm never shy about introducing ideas for improvements and process changes and my own two cents on how things should be done. But I don't think joining a new team is the time for that. Why do I think that is? Really from... Well I do have a background in training and development a little bit for a year or so. I hired and taught training classes at a nonprofit organization. And being on the other side of that situation, I always appreciated the trainees who were willing to do what I just said. content: Um, it depends.
